era_context: 1376 is a year of political transition and consolidation across multiple civilizations. In Western Europe, Edward III of England is aging and in declining health, and the question of succession is becoming pressing. The Hundred Years' War continues with reduced intensity. In China, the Ming Dynasty is consolidating power under the Hongwu Emperor. In the Ottoman Empire and the Balkans, Ottoman expansion continues. This is a year of relative stability before the major political transitions of 1377–1378.

events.title: Death of Edward, the Black Prince: Succession crisis in England
events.description: Edward, the Black Prince (Edward of Woodstock), the eldest son of Edward III and heir to the English throne, died on June 8, 1376. Edward the Black Prince had been one of the most celebrated English military commanders of the Hundred Years' War, known for his victories at Crécy (1346) and Poitiers (1356). His death created a succession crisis, as the direct line of succession was broken. The throne would now pass to his young son Richard (age 9), rather than to Edward III's surviving sons. This created political instability and set the stage for the conflicts of the 1380s. Edward III, now in his 60s and in declining health, had only a few more years to live.
events.certainty_note: The death of Edward the Black Prince is one of the most thoroughly documented events of the 14th century, with contemporary royal records and multiple chronicles providing detailed accounts. The date is certain.
cross_references: 1377-001 (Death of Edward III and succession of Richard II)

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English military operations and the question of succession
events.description: The Hundred Years' War continued in 1376 with English forces conducting military operations in France. The war had entered a phase of reduced intensity compared to the mid-14th century, with a series of truces, raids, and limited engagements rather than large-scale battles. In 1376, the English were attempting to maintain their territorial holdings in Aquitaine and Calais while conducting raids along the Channel coast. The death of Edward the Black Prince in June 1376 created uncertainty in English military strategy and raised questions about the future direction of the war under a new succession.
key_figures: Charles V of France (King of France)
events.certainty_note: The broader military context is well-documented; specific engagements in 1376 are attested in chronicles but lack contemporary military records.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Hongwu Emperor's administrative consolidation
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) continued his systematic consolidation of Ming power in 1376. The emperor was implementing administrative reforms designed to centralize authority and eliminate the power of regional warlords and the old Yuan bureaucracy. In this year, the Ming court was reorganizing provincial administration and establishing the institutional structures that would define Ming governance for centuries. The Hongwu Emperor was also consolidating military power and establishing the system of imperial examinations that would become central to Ming administration.
key_figures: Zhu Yuanzhang / Hongwu Emperor (Emperor of China)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Ming administrative records and the official Ming Shilu provide detailed documentation of the Hongwu Emperor's reign and consolidation efforts.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans: Murad I's military campaigns
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Murad I continued its expansion into the Balkans in 1376. Ottoman forces were consolidating control over territories conquered in previous years and preparing for further expansion. The Ottoman military system, based on the devshirme (levy of Christian boys for military service) and the elite Janissary corps, was becoming increasingly effective. By 1376, the Ottoman Empire was the dominant military power in the eastern Mediterranean and the Balkans, and Christian states in the region were increasingly fragmented and unable to mount effective resistance.
events.certainty_note: The broader context of Ottoman expansion is well-established, though specific events in 1376 are not well-documented in contemporary sources. Ottoman chronicles are often later compilations with uncertain dating.

events.title: Papal politics: Gregory XI consolidates papal authority
events.description: Pope Gregory XI (Pierre Roger) continued his efforts to consolidate papal authority in 1376. Gregory XI had been elected pope in 1370 and had moved the papal seat back to Rome from Avignon in 1377 (a decision that was being planned in 1376). This move was controversial among the cardinals, many of whom preferred the papal court to remain in Avignon. In 1376, the papal court was experiencing significant political tensions related to the question of the papal seat's location and the future direction of the Church. Gregory XI was also dealing with the Papal States' political and military challenges.
events.certainty_note: The broader context of papal politics is well-documented, though specific events in 1376 are not as thoroughly documented as later years. The move of the papal seat to Rome occurred in 1377, not 1376.

disconfirming_evidence: The death of Edward the Black Prince is sometimes presented as a catastrophic blow to English military power, but contemporary sources indicate that English military capabilities remained strong under other commanders, particularly John of Gaunt. The claim that the succession crisis created by Edward the Black Prince's death was a major turning point in English history is somewhat overstated; the political instability was significant but not unprecedented. Additionally, the significance of 1376 as a specific turning point is somewhat artificial; the political transitions of 1376–1378 were part of a longer process of change.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1376 is rich for Western Europe, particularly regarding the death of Edward the Black Prince, which is documented in royal records and multiple chronicles. The record is moderately detailed for France (Hundred Years' War) and for China (Ming administrative records). The record is sparse for the Ottoman Empire and the Balkans, where Ottoman sources are often later compilations with uncertain dating. The record is minimal for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, the Americas, and Oceania. The year is well-documented for elite political and military history but poorly documented for economic, social, and cultural history outside elite circles. There is a significant Western bias in the surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: The death of Edward the Black Prince in 1376 set the stage for the succession of Richard II in 1377
The succession crisis created by Edward the Black Prince's death contributed to the political instability that led to the Peasants' Revolt in 1381
The papal tensions in 1376 led to the move of the papal seat to Rome in 1377 and the Great Schism in 1378
